title: Exception Handling

content.question: What are the 3 most likely edge cases in your business's flow (refund, no-show, fraud), and do you have a written playbook for each?
sop: List your 5 most common edge cases from the last 90 days (refunds, no-shows, disputes, fraud, complaints).
Write a one-page playbook for each: trigger, first response, who owns it, when to escalate.
Put the playbooks somewhere your team can find them in under 30 seconds, not buried in your head.
Rate Speed, Accuracy, and Containment 1-10 for your last real edge case, and rate Chaos 1-10 based on how much it disrupted other work (see calculation, page 6)
After each new edge case, add it to the playbook so the list never stops growing.
content.explainer: Average-case thinking is where amateurs live. Pros design for the 5% of cases that create 95% of the pain.
questions: What are the 3 most likely edge cases in your business's flow (refund, no-show, fraud), and do you have a written playbook for each?
The last time an edge case hit, did your team know what to do, or did they wait for you?
Which 5% of situations are eating 95% of your team's troubleshooting time?

definitions.def: An unusual or extreme situation at the margins of normal operation (a refund, a no-show, fraud). Rare individually, but collectively they cause most of the pain.

content.equation_example: Rate Speed, Accuracy, and Containment 1-10 each, then rate Chaos 1-10. Speed 8, Accuracy 7, Containment 6, Chaos 3 → EHA = (8×7×6)/3 = 112. Higher EHA means edge cases get handled fast and cleanly without disrupting everything else; lower means one bad case turns into a fire drill.
content.closing_statement: Design for the exceptions, not the average day.

caption:
Principle Nº 17: Exception Handling

Edge cases kill systems.

Ask yourself: What are the 3 most likely edge cases in your business's flow (refund, no-show, fraud), and do you have a written playbook for each?
